filer: National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners
overall_summary: NARUC questions whether FERC has jurisdiction over large load interconnections and warns the ANOPR risks impinging on exclusive state authority over retail rates, resource adequacy, and local distribution. It urges FERC to slow down and schedule a January 2026 technical conference to build a consensus, cooperative-federalism solution, and lays out conditions if FERC proceeds to a NOPR, including flexibility for regional diversity, applying any rule only to RTO/ISO participants, NERC and OATT compliance, and making large load customers responsible for 100 percent of upgrade costs.

quotes.text: it seems that many of the commenters agree with NARUC that FERC’s proposed ANOPR impermissibly treads into the realm of state jurisdiction, or at the very least, raises significant uncertainties that may lead to protracted legal disputes.
quotes.concern: FERC's ANOPR intrudes on state jurisdiction and risks legal disputes

quotes.text: every single state public utility commission (“PUC”) of the nineteen state PUCs that filed comments
quotes.concern: All nineteen state PUCs that filed share the jurisdictional concern

quotes.text: The state PUCs stressed that Congress through the Federal Power Act created a bright-line jurisdictional divide between FERC and that the Commission must respect and honor that jurisdictional bright-line in any rule it issues.
quotes.concern: FPA sets a bright-line state-federal divide FERC must honor

quotes.text: NARUC requests that the Commission work closely with state PUCs on these issues.
quotes.concern: FERC should collaborate closely with state regulators

quotes.text: NARUC respectfully requests that the Commission schedule a two-day technical conference in January 2026, to explore viable solutions with key stakeholders.
quotes.concern: Requests a two-day technical conference in January 2026

quotes.text: Instead of issuing a rule that attempts to address all issues raised in the initial comments, NARUC urges the Commission to move quickly to schedule a technical conference.
quotes.concern: Slow the rulemaking; convene a conference instead of a rushed rule

quotes.text: If the Commission proposes reforms, the reforms should permit flexibility and should address the real challenges currently facing the interconnection of large load
quotes.concern: Any reforms should be flexible and address real challenges

quotes.text: Any rule should provide for flexibility and should account for regional diversity and market structure diversity.
quotes.concern: Rule must allow for regional and market structure diversity

quotes.text: Some commenters stress that any future rule should only apply to participants involved in a regional transmission organization or independent system operator.
quotes.concern: Any rule should apply only to RTO/ISO participants

quotes.text: Any rule must also ensure that all large load interconnections comply with NERC reliability standards and Open Access Transmission Tariff provisions, including technical requirements for system protection facilities for hybrid projects and potential penalties for unauthorized power injections or withdrawals.
quotes.concern: Require NERC and OATT compliance, protection facilities, and injection penalties

quotes.text: Any proposed rule should specify that large load customers are responsible for 100% of the upgrade costs consistent with the cost causation principle.
quotes.concern: Large load customers should bear 100 percent of upgrade costs

quotes.text: any rule must ensure that the seven-factor test is applied properly.
quotes.concern: The seven-factor jurisdictional test must be applied properly

bins.name: FERC jurisdiction over large loads
bins.description: NARUC questions FERC's authority over large load interconnections and says the ANOPR impermissibly treads into state jurisdiction over retail rates and services. It points to all nineteen state PUCs that filed sharing this concern, stresses the Federal Power Act's bright-line divide, asks FERC to work closely with state PUCs, and insists the seven-factor test be applied properly.

bins.name: Slow down, hold a conference
bins.description: NARUC opposes rushing to a rule that tries to resolve every issue and urges FERC instead to move quickly to convene a technical conference to find consensus solutions.

bins.name: Two-day January 2026 conference
bins.description: NARUC asks FERC to schedule a two-day technical conference in January 2026 so commissioners and stakeholders can explore consensus-based solutions together.

bins.name: Flexibility and regional diversity
bins.description: If FERC proposes reforms, NARUC wants them to permit flexibility, address the real challenges of large load interconnection, and account for regional and market structure diversity.

bins.name: Limit rule to RTO/ISO participants
bins.description: NARUC relays that any future rule should apply only to participants in a regional transmission organization or independent system operator.

bins.name: NERC and OATT compliance
bins.description: NARUC says any rule must ensure large load interconnections comply with NERC reliability standards and OATT provisions, including system protection requirements and penalties for unauthorized power injections or withdrawals.

bins.name: System protection for hybrid projects
bins.description: NARUC calls for technical requirements for system protection facilities for hybrid projects as part of any rule.

bins.name: Large loads pay 100 percent of upgrades
bins.description: NARUC supports specifying that large load customers are responsible for 100 percent of upgrade costs, consistent with the cost causation principle.

bins.name: Cost causation for upgrade costs
bins.description: NARUC grounds the upgrade-cost allocation in the cost causation principle, assigning the full cost of upgrades to the large load customers that cause them.
